text: Hi friends, this video demonstrates our technique of performing a laparoscopic ultra low anterior resection in a patient with a CA rectum post chemo radiotherapy.
The patient is positioned flat on the operating table, and the omentum is being carefully moved towards the stomach.
The surgeon tilts the surgical field to the left lateral position, causing small bubbles to fall away from the area being dissected.
The surgeon ensures that the entire ileum up to the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure is free, indicating the completion of the mobilization of this section of the intestine.
This is a good tip because we always use a gauze whenever there is some small oozing to clean up the field so that the light does not absorb

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and makes a small incision on the peritoneum covering the mesocolon of the sigmoid at the level of the sacral promontory, extending the incision on the peritoneum without going deeper.
The surgeon performs a superficial dissection, with carbon dioxide insufflation aiding in the opening of tissue planes. A small plane begins to visibly open up following the peritoneal incision.
The surgeon dissects towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), extending slightly beyond to remove the surrounding lymphovascular structures.
Based on the level of anastomosis,

our general preference is to preserve the left colic whenever the anastomosis is going to be at a slightly higher level and take down only the superior rectal and the hemorrhoidal artery but here because the anastomosis is quite low at the level of the pelvic floor we would like to remove the or ligate the ima at the root itself leaving just a couple of centimeters of the iota because the left colic even if preserved will have a lot of tension and it will not allow the to reach the anastomotic site without much of a tension.
The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction while carbon dioxide insufflation helps to open up the dissection planes, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a clear avascular plane and observes the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane opening up effectively, indicating successful dissection progress.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect tissue around the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), ensuring precise removal of lymphovascular structures.

We generally try to keep the wattage of the cautery quite low around twenty five watts to maximum of thirty watts so that the fumes generated would not be too much and unless we go deep into the narrow pelvis of a male we will not have too much of issues with the fumes coming into our field of dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving cranially towards the take-off area of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta, with visible tissue manipulation and exposure.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook or spatula to perform colorectal resections in an area without major vessels, relying on simple energy tools for dissection.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to further dissect the tissue, creating headway in the surgical field. Carbon dioxide insufflation visibly opens up the anatomical planes, guiding the direction of dissection.
The surgeon advances the dissection beyond the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, using short bursts of cautery to carefully separate tissue planes.
The surgeon makes an incision extending to the lower border of the pancreas to facilitate ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein at this level.
The surgeon identifies and works within the correct anatomical plane, distinguishing between two visible tissue planes during the dissection process.
The surgeon elevates the fat above the specimen, noting that both planes appear normal and avascular, while emphasizing the importance of distinguishing between them.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the superior rectal artery and carefully dissects close to the vascular sheath, maintaining proximity to the artery while navigating the surgical planes.
The surgeon pushes a vessel upward while working just below the vascular sheath, maintaining close proximity to the anatomical structure.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter before proceeding with dissection near the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring it is visible and protected prior to ligating the artery.

Or else during the initial phases of a carrier it is very easy to get carried away thinking the ureter as the inferior mesenteric artery and that can lead to a lot of injuries which we can totally avoid if we are familiar with this small awareness in the anatomy and as well as the technique in identifying the ureter first and only then ligating.
The surgeon navigates within the Total Mesorectal Excision (TME) plane, known as the 'holy plane' described by Professor Bill Heald, ensuring precise dissection along this anatomical layer.

The surgeon navigates between the peritoneum of the viscera and the presacral fascia, avoiding the deeper plane where pre-cyclical vessels run to prevent injury to small venules. The ureter is visibly distant from the dissection field, and care is taken to ensure the incision of the peritoneum does not approach the ureter.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully maneuvering to avoid injury to the ureter, ensuring it remains safe and undisturbed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving inferiorly while maintaining the correct plane, and progresses cranially towards the take-off of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to aid in dissection, carefully progressing through the correct anatomical planes. The hook is used to coagulate thin tissue layers incrementally, avoiding thick, bulky tissue coagulation.

The surgeon identifies a vessel indicating they have gone a layer deeper than intended. They adjust the dissection to return to the correct anatomical plane, moving inferiorly to ensure proper identification of the surgical plane.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is covered by a thin fascia that must be preserved to prevent exposure of the ureters and gonadal vessels. The dissection is adjusted to ensure the ureter remains covered, indicating the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to take down small vessels and nerve branches running towards the colon, ensuring no major vessels are present in the dissection area.

The surgeon works posterior to the vascular sheath pedicle, reaching the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). A vessel sealer is used to divide and seal lymphatic channels, aiming to reduce postoperative lymphorrhea.

The surgeon carefully dissects around a visible vessel, removing lymphovascular structures from the base of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) over the aorta. Attention is given to avoid exposing the aorta excessively to prevent injury to the hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon carefully dissects beyond the level of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) takeoff, preserving the surrounding plexus while removing lymphovascular tissue.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal and dissect tissues, moving beyond the vessel. Small branches leading to the mesocolon of the descending colon are visible. The vessel is clearly exposed, and a right angle or Maryland dissector is used to maneuver around it.

The surgeon navigates around the vessel with minimal intervening structures in the dissection field. A clip is applied to secure the vessel, followed by the use of a vessel sealer to ensure hemostasis, with cold scissors as an alternative option.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to secure the vessel, followed by applying a clip or ligature. In cases with limited pedicle availability, cold scissors are used for dissection. The vessel is carefully managed due to potential atherosclerosis in elderly patients, minimizing post-operative bleeding risks.

The surgeon exposes the fascia covering the ureter, with the gonadal vessels visible in the surgical field. The peritoneum is clearly delineated, and the line of Toldt gradually appears in the area of dissection. The psoas muscle is briefly visible.

The surgeon identifies the preserved fascia in an avascular plane, with the psoas muscle visible in the background. The instrument points to the bluish tubular structure of the gonadal vein, highlighting the thin fascia and the precise dissection of anatomical planes.

The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle and proceeds with dissection towards the lateral aspect, aiming to reach the limit of dissection from the medial side. An incision is made on the peritoneum to open the planes, continuing the incision close to the lower border of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the bluish tubular structure as the inferior mesenteric vein towards the left of the screen, working near the duodenojejunal flexure. Peritoneal attachments are visible, and the small bowel is carefully maneuvered out of the field using an assisting hand to avoid deep dissection and potential nerve damage.

The surgeon cuts through a thin peritoneal layer, then holds and lifts the inferior mesenteric vein to develop the avascular plane inferior to the inferior mesenteric artery. An instrument is placed below the vein to lift it further, clearly identifying the vessel.

The surgeon carefully pushes down the peritoneum covering Gerota's fascia, ensuring the perinephric pad of fat remains covered by fascia. Despite the thinness of the fascia, the dissection is performed meticulously, maintaining clear anatomical planes without excessive tissue staining.
The surgeon uses a blunt suction instrument to meticulously dissect the thin peritoneum away from the fascia covering the mesocolon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the CME plane during colon resection, highlighting the avascular space between the Gerota's fascia and the mesocolon.

The surgeon uses blunt traction to meticulously push the fascia, continuing dissection towards the left shoulder until the spleen becomes visible. The pancreas is also starting to appear in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an instrument to point out the pancreas, which is clearly visible due to the patient's lack of visceral obesity. The tissues are being gently pushed aside without holding them, allowing for clear identification of the pancreas.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the splenic flexure from the medial side, preparing to use one of three techniques: medial to lateral, lateral to medial, or cranial to caudal.
The surgeon prepares for an anastomosis at the pelvic floor by ligating the inferior mesenteric vein at the lower border of the pancreas, ensuring sufficient colon length for the procedure.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein at the lower border of the pancreas, ligates it, and uses a sealer to divide it. The mesentery above the pancreas is divided, revealing the surface of the pancreas as the attachments are released.

The surgeon performs dissection at the lower border of the pancreas, gradually revealing the anterior surface of the pancreas. The border of the pancreas is identified, and the surgeon stays slightly away from it while incising and releasing attachments for proper splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon tunnels below the mesentery on top of the pancreas, entering the lesser sac. The stomach is visible, along with the white space of the lesser sac. Vessels are seen running in the gastrocolic omentum, and the transverse colon is visible. The surgeon continues incising attachments laterally towards the left side, approaching the spleen.

The surgeon makes an incision just medial to the line of Toldt, revealing the dissected plane inferior to the area. The blue gonadal vessel is visible, with the fascia covering the gonadals preserved.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide to open the surgical plane and continues to increase the incision on the peritoneum to release the colon. Small flimsy attachments are addressed with blunt and sharp dissection, particularly near the splenic flexure area from the lateral aspect. The inferior and posterior areas of the mesocolon have already been dissected, highlighting the advantage of a medial to lateral approach in patients with visceral obesity.
The surgeon uses a monopolar device to effectively mobilize the splenic flexure from a medial to lateral approach, progressing towards the splenic flexure area.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to open up the dissection planes, revealing small thin fascial attachments. Once these attachments are released, the descending colon appears free due to prior dissection on the medial and inferior aspects.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection of the splenic flexure, using traction and counter traction to release small fibrous tissues. The colon is progressively freed as flimsy structures are separated with sharp and blunt dissection, without encountering any thick vascular structures.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to rapidly release adhesions, progressing towards the splenic flexure area. The avascular dissection planes are clearly identified, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon uses traction to open up thin facial planes, facilitating rapid and avascular dissection. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, previously released from the medial aspect, with visible slight blood tinge indicating the area of prior dissection.

The surgeon releases a small fibrous strand, revealing a gauze marker placed on the medial side to guide the dissection. The gauze is now clearly visible, indicating the correct dissection plane. The surgeon continues to remove small, thin attachments to enhance the mobility of the colon from the splenic flexure area.

The spleen becomes visible in the surgical field as the surgeon identifies dissection planes near the splenic flexure. Using a cautery device, the surgeon rapidly dissects the remaining thin fibrous tissues, freeing the area around the kidney and spleen. The colon is released from flimsy attachments, completing the dissection from the medial side.

The surgeon releases omental attachments towards the splenic flexure and distal portion of the transverse mesocolon, ensuring the omentum does not exert a tugging effect on the bowel. The lateral attachments are visibly released, and further dissection of the gastrocolic omentum towards the middle of the transverse mesocolon is performed to achieve comprehensive mobilization.

The surgeon uses a monopolar energy source to continue dissection, with a small bipolar device available for managing small bleeders from retracting mesenteric vessels. A sealer is used to expedite dissection in three specific areas of the left colon: near the inferior mesenteric area for sealing lymphovascular vessels, during gastrocolic omentum division, and another unspecified area.

The surgeon identifies the area where the middle rectal artery may be present, noting its larger size. A vessel sealer is used to manage this vessel. The lateral incision is extended cranially and caudally, revealing the TME plane. The incision on the peritoneum opens up the dissection plane, allowing for straightforward continuation of the procedure.

The surgeon progresses the total mesorectal excision (TME) by entering the true pelvic space, working below the sacral promontory. The dissection is performed close to the mesocolon and mesorectum, avoiding the presacral fascia to maintain the correct avascular plane.

The surgeon works close to the mesorectum, revealing the left hypogastric nerve clearly visible and preserved. The nerve, approximately two millimeters in size, is easily identifiable in the clean surgical field. The ureter is also visible, covered by fascia.

The surgeon performs dissection on the lateral part, leaving the peritoneum intact. The peritoneum is ready to be incised, similar to the previous lateral incision for mobilizing the descending colon. A combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques is used to achieve precise dissection.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect near the pelvic sidewall, carefully pushing the visible nerves away from the field of dissection. The nerves are covered by a thin fascial plane, and their course is clearly visible, allowing for precise preservation during the procedure.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction near the pelvic sidewall, enhancing the visual appreciation of the anatomical planes. Careful dissection is performed close to the nerve, ensuring the correct plane is identified and preserved.

The surgeon increases traction and counter-traction to open up the dissection planes, revealing the internal iliac vein and ureter in close proximity. Carbon dioxide insufflation aids in maintaining the correct dissection plane, ensuring careful navigation to avoid injury to these structures.

The surgeon identifies familiar anatomical areas and begins dissection in the correct plane, using traction and counter traction to guide the process. A plane opens up, revealing the correct direction for further dissection.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to clearly visualize and dissect within the correct anatomical planes, avoiding deep dissection in these areas.

The surgeon maintains close proximity to the mesorectum during dissection, avoiding excessive inferior movement. As traction is released, the fascia visibly falls away and appears fused with the pelvic sidewall or sacrum hollow.

The surgeon continues to define and incise the tissue planes, working anteriorly, anterolaterally, and posteriorly. The tissues are carefully incised at the precise level to avoid harm, with the planes becoming more clearly defined.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the plane, preserving the thin fascia, which remains intact. The posterior dissection is completed before proceeding to the lateral aspects, with the option to adjust the sequence based on preference or patient-specific factors.

The main thing that we have to keep in mind is to identify the right planes and make sure that we are sticking to them. At the same time we progress and we are rather not stuck in only one level. Because in a male patient the pelvis is very narrow, the mesentery and the bowel is bulky and it is very technically challenging to perform a good TME. Whereas here with the good clear vision and the field which we have maintained without much of oozing,

The surgeon continues the total mesorectal excision (TME) with clear visualization of the anatomical planes. The presacral area shows minimal fat, and the vessels are prominently visible. The mesorectum is clearly identifiable as the dissection progresses smoothly.

The surgeon identifies a nerve located to the left of the dissection area within the surrounding fat. Carbon dioxide insufflation is visibly expanding the tissue planes, aiding in the dissection near the pelvic floor. Fumes from the procedure temporarily obscure the view, requiring a pause for them to clear. A large vessel, likely the median sacral vessel, is visible in the midline, with the pelvic side wall seen on the left.

The surgeon continues dissection along the pelvic side wall, approaching the curvature of the pelvic side wall. The dissection progresses from the posterior aspect towards the lateral side, ensuring the posterior dissection is complete before moving to the lateral and anterior aspects.

The surgeon performs precise dissection over the pelvic floor, with visible muscle contraction occurring during cautery use. The dissection progresses towards the lateral side, facilitated by traction and counter traction, revealing well-defined tissue planes. Carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening the planes and guiding the dissection path.

The surgeon stretches the tissues to provide traction and counter traction, clearly identifying small nerve branches and a thick vessel, the middle rectal artery, supplying the rectum. The dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor and moves anteriorly.

The surgeon progresses the dissection from the inferior aspect towards the lateral and anterior sides, approaching the anterior aspect without incising the peritoneum. A vessel sealer is used on a vessel in the middle rectal area, which is variable in anatomy and may be small or absent in some patients.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to guide the dissection, preserving a small fascia and some fat over the seminal vesicle on the right side to protect the nerves. The peritoneum is incised anteriorly, leaving a centimeter intact to ensure dissection in the correct plane.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to coagulate small vessels near the right seminal vesicle, ensuring the fascia covering remains intact to avoid exposing the seminal vesicle. The dissection proceeds below the level of the prostate, with the prostate expected to become visible shortly.

The surgeon navigates around the prostate, visible near the instrument, aiming to include the Denonvilliers fascia with the specimen for additional anterior margin. The dissection proceeds between the layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, leaving the prostate exposed while providing an extra margin due to the anterior tumor location.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, mirroring the previous right-side approach. The pelvic sidewall is visible inferiorly, with the levator muscles clearly identifiable. The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to incise a small fused portion of the peritoneum in the midline, progressing the dissection superiorly.

The surgeon uses cautery to release the peritoneum, revealing the prostate more clearly. The dissection progresses on the left side, with visible differentiation between the fat covering the mesorectum and the fat surrounding the seminal vesicle. Traction and counter-traction techniques are employed to facilitate precise dissection.

The surgeon identifies the avascular plane for dissection, minimizing bleeding as they progress. The dissection continues until reaching the end of the Denonvilliers fascia, which is incised to maintain the correct plane by pushing the fascia onto the specimen.

The surgeon works close to the left seminal vesicle, using short bursts of cautery and a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The pelvic side wall is clearly visible, with twitching levator muscles observed near the lateral portion. The inferior portion has been well dissected, revealing small fascial strands between the pelvic side wall and the rectum. Further dissection is required as the surgeon moves anteriorly.

The surgeon releases attachments around the specimen, aiming for a cylindrical dissection. Short bursts of cautery are used to dissect in the correct plane, visibly opening up the tissue planes as the dissection progresses in a circular manner.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, revealing the prostate's shape as it appears in the surgical field. Using short bursts of cautery, the surgeon works to join the dissection spaces on the left and right sides, ensuring they remain in the correct plane with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the lateral aspect, using traction and counter traction to reveal thin fascial planes. The remaining attachments are dissected near the pelvic side wall, with a small branch of a vessel visible, which will be addressed shortly.
The surgeon coagulates a small bleeder on the prostate aspect while applying counter traction to better identify the anatomical planes for precise dissection.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to further dissect towards the inferior portion of the bladder, revealing the Denonvilliers fascia clearly. The fascia is taken along with the specimen during the dissection for a posteriorly located tumor.

The surgeon dissects above the denonvillia fascia, leaving it attached to the prostate to prevent it from becoming bare. Small vessels on the prostate are visible, and a thick taut tissue is observed on the rectal surface. The dissection aims to take an additional anterior margin for a clear circumferential resection margin (CRM).

The surgeon exposes the taut denonvillia fascia, clearly visible in the surgical field. Care is taken to avoid injury to the seminal vesicle by adjusting the dissection plane laterally, ensuring the fascia is pulled down appropriately.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral portion using gentle traction and cautery to manage small venules and prevent bleeding, ensuring a clear surgical field.

The surgeon works close to the Denonvilliers fascia, identifying the pulsating branches of the middle rectal vessels, which need to be carefully dissected and taken down as they supply the middle part of the rectum.

The surgeon uses a tissue sealer to dissect inflamed tissues in the surgical area. Lymphatic fluid and tissue fluid are visible, resembling blood oozing and collecting in the field. A gauze is placed over the area to manage the fluid, with intermittent suction applied to the gauze.

The surgeon performs further dissection on the right side, encountering edematous tissue that generates smoke due to electrocautery use. The surgical field is maintained clean by ensuring the hook tip is free of tissue debris, allowing for precise dissection despite the challenging anatomy in a male patient.

very good results for the patient which would be totally worth the procedure and as well as the maximum benefit can be offered to the patient. But we have to always remember that it is not only the surgery which can lead to all this, even radiation and the chemotherapy which we have can really cause some amount of damage to these nerves. So, the results will never be as good as in a normal patient.

The surgeon meticulously manages small oozes and blood vessels to ensure hemostasis. The pelvic side wall on the right side becomes visible, similar to the left side, as the dissection continues to further isolate the rectum.

The surgeon carefully works near the prostate, visible just above the hook, while pushing down the thick, white Denonvilliers fascia. As the dissection progresses caudally, the fascia is incised to transition to a normal plane, avoiding nerve and urethral injury.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy to sharply incise the denonvillia fascia, transitioning back to the plane of the mesorectum located just below the fascia.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis while dissecting through small thin fibrous structures in a deeper plane than typically used for total mesorectal excision (TME). The denonvillia fascia is clearly visible as a white taut tissue.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, incising the denonvillia fascia at this level. Slight oozing is visible as the incision progresses from non-standard planes into the correct anatomical plane, revealing the right plane beneath the fascia.

The surgeon has dissected inferior to the thick, taut Denonvilliers fascia, leaving it visible at the top of the surgical field. The surrounding fat is seen as free from the fascia. The assisting hand holds a suction device to manage fumes in the narrow surgical area.

The surgeon intermittently uses suction to clear the field, revealing the cylindrical incision of the Denonvilliers fascia on the anterior aspect. The incision is extended laterally, creating a clear dissection plane. Small venules or vessels are visible but do not pose a concern for significant bleeding.

So I hope all of you were quite clear with the anatomy over here because this is very important and will come in handy for you when you have anterior tumors especially in those where there is a threatening of the circumferential resection margin. So this would act as a small buffer giving you more negative margin and better quality of life for the patient as well if you could achieve it and offer it to him in a proper manner.

The surgeon uses cautery to sharply incise the edge of the denonvillia fascia, progressing laterally. The incised edge is visible alongside the continuation of the fascia on the lateral aspect. Suction is used to remove fumes generated by the cautery, improving visibility in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues dissection in a thin, clearly visible plane using a hook cautery, which has some charred tissue adhering to its tip. Despite the potential for large fume generation, the dissection proceeds smoothly, with the option to clean the instrument to reduce fume production.

The surgeon examines the edge of the incised Denonvillier's fascia, identifying the intact area that requires further incision. The focus is on avoiding lateral extension beyond the seminal vesicles to prevent nerve injuries.

The surgeon completes the incision of the Denonvilliers fascia, dividing the last portion. The surrounding mesorectal fat is visible, allowing rapid development of the surgical plane without concern for small vessels.

A lot of people nowadays claim that only with robotic you get a very good precision and we will be able to do all these complicated tasks in a meticulous manner, but I would tend to disagree. The robotic, yes, does have its own advantages. It gives you a very nice stable platform for the surgeon and it gives you a lot of flexibility. It is less turn-averse on the surgeon to perform the surgery. but nevertheless you can offer the same in laparoscopy as well even though it is a little more cumbersome for the operating surgeon.

Right now the operative costs with the robotics are quite expensive and I don't think it's suitable for the Indian scenario yet for most of the patients. for some patients who have a pretty good insurance coverage and they don't really mind about spending it and then for them it does make sense to do it robotically because it's way less turnovers for the surgeon but even patients who are not really able to afford robotic surgery can really

get the benefit of a minimal access surgery when a good laparoscopic surgery is done but the care is that the good laparoscopic surgery has to be done and there is no way we can compromise on this principle if we have any difficulty through which we are not able to do this TME in a similar kind of a manner we have to definitely convert there is no point in doing a suboptimal surgery that would in turn really relate to a very poor prognosis for the

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect the last adherent portion of the Denonvillier's fascia around the rectum, progressing towards the pelvic floor. The rectum is being carefully dissected circumferentially at the level of the pelvic floor.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) by releasing the remaining strands of the Denonvilliers fascia at the pelvic floor. Traction and counter-traction techniques are used to open up the tissue planes, facilitating meticulous dissection around the middle rectum.

The surgeon identifies the target area for dissection, addressing small oozes using short bursts of monopolar cautery. For more persistent oozing from small venules, bipolar cautery is employed to effectively seal the vessels.

The surgeon identifies the exact location of the Denonvilliers fascia and works below it to achieve a comprehensive dissection. The tip of the instrument is cleaned, and fewer fumes are generated as the thin tissues are dissected.

The surgeon navigates a true avascular plane, performing a cylindrical dissection around the rectum. The tissue appears less inflamed and thick, allowing for a straightforward surgical approach without swapping between planes.

It is in these patients where the pelvis is very narrow and we do not have much of a working space to perform that a trans-anal TME also would come into view or a trans-anal minimal invasive surgery like a thymus can come in to help us in performing the dissection very well because even though there is a low working space when we come from the anal end it is quite easy to really do this dissection but the only flip side is you need some special insufflators for that otherwise it becomes very cumbersome to do a thymus

And also you need another person who is capable enough to do a pretty decent job on the abdominal side so that you can start performing both the procedures in a concomitant manner. If you try to do one procedure and come down to the next then that is only going to add up to the operative time and it is going to become more difficult for you to do it.

The surgeon has reached the pelvic floor and is isolating the minimal fat surrounding the rectum to expose it fully. A stapler is prepared to divide the rectum. A digital rectal examination is performed to check for tumor palpability, confirming that the dissection has progressed beyond the tumor site. The assistant attempts to palpate the tumor but is unable to do so. A green 45-degree roticulating stapler is used in the procedure.

The surgeon uses a 45mm stapler to divide the rectal stem, achieving a clean separation. A small incision is made, and the specimen is pulled outside. The inferior mesenteric pedicle is excised, and the mesentery is coned for further dissection.

The surgeon incises the colon and creates a purse string suture. The anvil is placed into the purse string, and an end-to-end anastomosis is prepared. The spike is brought through the divided portion of the rectum, visible beyond the pelvic floor. The surgeon uses a stapler to push the spike into position, holding beyond the groove on the anvil to lock both instruments together.

The surgeon removes any loose sutures at the junction and locks the instruments until the orange line disappears, indicating proper alignment. The orientation of the bowel is checked to ensure there is no twisting, and the stapler is closed to perform the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and insufflates air with saline into the pelvis to check for any leakage, observing the surgical site for signs of fluid escape.
